preamble: The development of a voiced dental stop [d] into a voiced postalveolar affricate [dʒ] often occurs via yod-coalescence (d + j > dʒ). This process is widespread in the history of English and Romance languages, where the dental articulation is pulled back toward the palate by a following glide or front vowel. It involves fronting and affrication triggered by a high front vowel or palatal glide.

examples.note: Historical yod-coalescence where the stop and glide fused.
Synchronic coalescent palatalization in rapid speech.
Historical palatalization of the dental stop before a glide.
Geminate palatalization where Latin /dj/ sequence results in Italian affricate.
Allophonic palatalization before high front vowel /i/.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Alkire, T., & Rosen, C. (2010). Romance Philology.
Wells, J. C. (1982). Accents of English.
